en: Are you learning British English and want to sound more natural when you speak?
Well, in this video, I'm going to teach you 16 real English expressions that
native British people use every single day. And in fact, this is a list of expressions that I use
pretty much all the time. And these are expressions most learners never learn in school. Now,
these aren't boring textbook phrases. They are daily British English phrases that you'll hear
at work, in shops, in casual conversations, and even in British TV shows and series. So,
whether you're preparing for life in the UK, you're trying to improve your speaking skills,
or you just want to speak English more fluently and confidently, then this lesson is for you.
Hello, you wonderful people. Welcome to English Right Now. My name is Roy,
your British English teacher. Now, I've been a teacher of English for nearly 15 years,
and I've helped people from all over the world on their journey towards mastering British English.
And on this channel, we talk about things like pronunciation in a modern British RP accent,
we talk about British English, and of course, British culture. And today, we're talking about

that we tend to use every day. And if you want to learn even more about this lesson,

in this lesson, we're going to go through the 16 phrases, we'll talk about what each of the

you're right. You're right. This is a casual greeting. You're right. And it isn't really
asking, are you sick? Are you unwell? It's more about saying, hi, you're right, is a standard
kind of replacement for hi. So, if you're in the street, and you see somebody, you might say,
you're right. And the common reply to this is, you're right. So, you're right. You're right.

you're all right. You're right. Number two is fancier. Fancier. This basically means,
do you want a. So, we should really say, do you fancier? Like, do you fancier a cup of tea,
or do you fancier going out for dinner? But quite commonly, we just get rid of that do you,
and just make it fancier. So, for example, oh, fancier pint later on, or fancier cuppa. So,
notice the pronunciation of the word a, the article is a. So, it becomes fancier. Fancier.
By the way, I said fancier cuppa. Cuppa means cup of tea. It's a slang version of the words

I'm just popping out, it means leaving the house for a short period of time. Maybe you're going to
the shop, the post office, into town, maybe. I'm just popping out to grab some milk. Now,
commonly, people will drop that sound at the end, and it becomes more like popping, popping,

and I don't feel like it. So, or should we do the washing up now? No, I can't be bothered. The way
we would pronounce this, first of all, the word can't, we drop the t because it's sandwiched
between a n and a b. So, it becomes can't be, can't be, can't be bothered, can't be bothered.
And notice that long ah vowel sound in the word can't. So, it's can't be bothered, can't be
bothered with that dropping of that t. A slightly ruder version of, well, it is actually quite a bit
version of this is, I can't be arsed. This has the same meaning. I wouldn't use this

it's slightly rude to say you're not in the mood. So, I wouldn't even use this at work. So,
if your boss comes over and goes, oh, could you do that report? No, I can't be bothered.

Sometimes we kind of use it for buy as well. This is classic. We use it everywhere. Particularly,

it's cheers. Please let me know in the comments which of these expressions is your favourite.

I said that I'm easy is also an alternative. Be careful with this one, because I'm easy can mean

either way, or I'm not fussed either way, is a politer alternative to express the same thing.

But a bit of a nightmare. We often use it to dramatize a quite trivial situation. So, oh,
